evals.name: feature-split-routing
evals.prompt: I want to add a CSV export option to the reporting module of my web app. There is an existing PDF export service under src/services/export/ that the new CSV exporter should mirror, it needs to be wired into the export menu component AND the report detail view, and the user-facing docs page needs updating. Split this work between multiple agents and show me the plan first.
evals.expected_output: A decomposition with an agent plan table: exporter design/implementation on the session model (no override), the docs update routed to Sonnet, a Codex cross-model review pass, phased fan-out (not a team), the shared export-menu/report-view touchpoints owned by one agent, and a wait for approval because the run mutates files in parallel.
expectations: Plan contains a per-subtask routing table (or equivalent) assigning a model/tier to each agent
Judgment-heavy work (exporter design/implementation, final synthesis) is left on the session model with no model override
The mechanical docs update is routed to Sonnet
Plan includes a Codex/GPT cross-model review pass on the implementation
Topology is phased fan-out, not a persistent team and not TeamCreate (which does not exist)

The two shared wiring points (export menu component, report detail view) are assigned to a single owner or to the orchestrator's own synthesis step, rather than split across two agents editing them concurrently
The plan waits for the user's approval before launching, on its own initiative — the prompt never tells it to stop

evals.name: audit-batching-and-sizing
evals.prompt: Audit all API route handlers under src/api/ in this repo for missing tenant scoping (handlers that should go through the requireTenant middleware but don't). There are about 40 handler files. Fan out agents to cover it — give me your orchestration plan.
evals.expected_output: A plan with a small batched fan-out (roughly 4-6 scanners partitioned over the 40 files, not one agent per file), cheap scanning on Sonnet, suspected findings confirmed by the orchestrator itself rather than by spawned verifier agents, and an explicit coverage statement.
expectations: Scanner agents are batched over the file set — roughly 4-8 agents partitioned across the ~40 files, NOT one agent per file and NOT an unbounded fan-out
Cheap scanning/grepping work is routed to Sonnet, or to a read-only Explore agent
Suspected findings are confirmed against the code by the orchestrator in its own loop before being reported as fact
The plan does NOT spawn separate verifier/adversarial-review subagents to check the scanners' findings
Plan addresses coverage explicitly (what will and will not be covered, or how completeness is ensured)

evals.name: small-run-no-over-gating
evals.prompt: /dynamic-agents Research how three competitors — Linear, Height, and Shortcut — each handle recurring or repeating tasks in their product: which pricing tier it lands in, the UX for setting one up, and the known limitations. Give me a comparison table at the end.
evals.expected_output: A compact plan (three parallel research legs on Sonnet, synthesis by the orchestrator), then an immediate launch — no approval checkpoint, because this is a small, explicitly invoked, read-only fan-out with no file mutation.
expectations: A compact plan is shown before launching
The three research legs run in parallel as separate agents
The research legs are routed to Sonnet (a defined question), not left on the session model
The run launches immediately after showing the plan — it does NOT stop to ask for approval or permission
The final comparison table is synthesized by the orchestrator itself rather than delegated to a subagent

evals.name: declines-fanout-not-worth-it
evals.prompt: I need to rename the `isActive` prop to `isEnabled` across three React components and update their tests. Worth fanning this out across dynamic agents? Give me your recommendation, and the plan if it's worth it.
evals.expected_output: A recommendation against fanning out: the task is small enough that writing self-contained briefs costs more than doing it inline. At most one agent, no Workflow, no team, no Codex review pass.
expectations: Recommends against fanning this out across multiple agents
Gives the reason in cost terms — briefing/orchestration overhead exceeds the work itself, or one agent can finish it inline
Proposes at most one agent (or doing it directly in the main loop)
Does NOT propose the Workflow tool, a persistent team, or worktree isolation
Does NOT propose a Codex cross-model review pass for a change this small

evals.name: hard-node-escalation-and-gate
evals.prompt: We're migrating auth from session cookies to JWT across a Next.js app. The token/refresh/rotation design is the genuinely hard part — we have SSR, edge middleware, and a mobile client sharing the same API, and getting rotation wrong logs everyone out. On top of that there are about 15 route handlers to mechanically update to the new helper, plus an auth docs page. Plan this across agents.
evals.expected_output: The token/rotation design stays on the session model or is escalated to Fable — never Sonnet. The ~15 route-handler updates are batched to Sonnet. Shared middleware/config is owned by one agent. A Codex cross-model review pass covers the security-sensitive implementation, and the run gates for approval before mutating files in parallel.
expectations: The token/refresh/rotation design is routed to the session model or escalated to Fable (`model: "fable"`) — it is NOT routed to Sonnet
If Fable is proposed, it is justified as an escalation for the single hardest node, not applied to every subtask
The ~15 mechanical route-handler updates are routed to Sonnet and batched across a few agents rather than one agent per handler
Shared/cross-cutting files (edge middleware, auth config) are reserved to a single owner or to the orchestrator, not edited by parallel agents
Plan includes a Codex/GPT cross-model review pass, justified by the security-sensitive nature of the change
The plan waits for the user's approval before launching, on its own initiative — the prompt never tells it to stop
